Journal: Neurocomputing

DOI: 10.1016/j.neucom.2026.133626

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

Multi-label classification is an important task in computer vision with many real-world applications. Recent works try to leverage rich knowledge from large pre-trained models ( e.g. , CLIP, ChatGPT) to tackle the issue by generating diverse label descriptions to enrich semantic information and matching image patches with predefined labels. However, due to obscured and small-sized objects under complex scenarios, existing methods suffer from misclassification and omission. Meanwhile, such methods mostly ignore the role of contextual scenario, which plays an important role in recognizing the objects in given images. To tackle the issues, we propose a new pipeline via multi-agent cooperation. Specifically, the pipeline consists of two stages: Look and Check. In the first stage, a multi-modal large model ( e.g. , LLaVA) is used as a looker, to search for objects in the image by a group of location-guided instructions. In the second stage, a large language model ( e.g. , LLaMA) serves as a checker, which is applied to understand the scenario and make inferring to assist the looker. Finally, the predicted results can be obtained by summarizing the two stages. Extensive experiments are constructed, demonstrating the superior performance of our method on four widely used benchmarks.

item.title: Diff-AgentDOA: Diffusion-agent Model for Synthetic Data Generation in Shallow-water Target Direction of Arrival Estimation
item.dc:creator: Jiawen He
Bin Zhang
Wenxu Wang
Peishun Liu
Liang Wang
Ruichun Tang
Hui Yu
item.description: Direction of arrival (DOA) estimation is a crucial pre-task for achieving passive target localization. The performance of DOA estimation, particularly in shallow-water environments, is severely limited by two challenges: insufficient labeled data and complex multipath propagation, which restrict model generalization and introduce angle ambiguity, respectively. Signal model-based DOA estimation methods do not rely on large amounts of labeled data and preserve physical consistency. However, when the model assumptions do not match the actual acoustic field, the DOA estimation accuracy is considerably reduced. Data-driven deep learning (DL) models regard multipath propagation as statistical disturbances in multi-sensor array data rather than as physical paths that must be explicitly separated, thereby breaking the ideal hypothesis constraints of signal models. Despite advances in DL, the scarcity of labeled data continues to hinder progress in underwater acoustic array processing. Thus, we propose a diffusion-agent model-based framework for shallow-water targets DOA estimation, named Diff-AgentDOA. Diff-AgentDOA comprises a diffusion-agent model for generating physically consistent synthetic data and a causal convolutional regression neural network (CausalCRNN) for DOA estimation. Extensive experiments on real sea trials, synthetic and simulated datasets, demonstrate that Diff-AgentDOA achieves an average relative reduction in DOA estimation error of 81% compared with signal model-based methods and 28% compared with the state-of-the-art DL methods. These results indicate the effectiveness of physically guided diffusion modeling and highlight the importance of synthetic-real label alignment. Published: 2026-04-11T08:34:00+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.779 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jiawen He; Bin Zhang; Wenxu Wang; Peishun Liu; Liang Wang; Ruichun Tang; Hui Yu

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104371

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

Direction of arrival (DOA) estimation is a crucial pre-task for achieving passive target localization. The performance of DOA estimation, particularly in shallow-water environments, is severely limited by two challenges: insufficient labeled data and complex multipath propagation, which restrict model generalization and introduce angle ambiguity, respectively. Signal model-based DOA estimation methods do not rely on large amounts of labeled data and preserve physical consistency. However, when the model assumptions do not match the actual acoustic field, the DOA estimation accuracy is considerably reduced. Data-driven deep learning (DL) models regard multipath propagation as statistical disturbances in multi-sensor array data rather than as physical paths that must be explicitly separated, thereby breaking the ideal hypothesis constraints of signal models. Despite advances in DL, the scarcity of labeled data continues to hinder progress in underwater acoustic array processing. Thus, we propose a diffusion-agent model-based framework for shallow-water targets DOA estimation, named Diff-AgentDOA. Diff-AgentDOA comprises a diffusion-agent model for generating physically consistent synthetic data and a causal convolutional regression neural network (CausalCRNN) for DOA estimation. Extensive experiments on real sea trials, synthetic and simulated datasets, demonstrate that Diff-AgentDOA achieves an average relative reduction in DOA estimation error of 81% compared with signal model-based methods and 28% compared with the state-of-the-art DL methods. These results indicate the effectiveness of physically guided diffusion modeling and highlight the importance of synthetic-real label alignment.

item.title: Scal3R: Scalable Test-Time Training for Large-Scale 3D Reconstruction
item.dc:creator: Tao Xie
Peishan Yang
Yudong Jin
Yingfeng Cai
Wei Yin
Weiqiang Ren
Qian Zhang
Wei Hua
Sida Peng
Xiaoyang Guo
Xiaowei Zhou
item.description: This paper addresses the task of large-scale 3D scene reconstruction from long video sequences. Recent feed-forward reconstruction models have shown promising results by directly regressing 3D geometry from RGB images without explicit 3D priors or geometric constraints. However, these methods often struggle to maintain reconstruction accuracy and consistency over long sequences due to limited memory capacity and the inability to effectively capture global contextual cues. In contrast, humans can naturally exploit the global understanding of the scene to inform local perception. Motivated by this, we propose a novel neural global context representation that efficiently compresses and retains long-range scene information, enabling the model to leverage extensive contextual cues for enhanced reconstruction accuracy and consistency. The context representation is realized through a set of lightweight neural sub-networks that are rapidly adapted during test time via self-supervised objectives, which substantially increases memory capacity without incurring significant computational overhead. The experiments on multiple large-scale benchmarks, including the KITTI Odometry~\cite{Geiger2012CVPR} and Oxford Spires~\cite{tao2025spires} datasets, demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in handling ultra-large scenes, achieving leading pose accuracy and state-of-the-art 3D reconstruction accuracy while maintaining efficiency. Code is available at https://zju3dv.github.io/scal3r. Published: 2026-04-09T17:59:50+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.779 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tao Xie; Peishan Yang; Yudong Jin; Yingfeng Cai; Wei Yin; Weiqiang Ren; Qian Zhang; Wei Hua; Sida Peng; Xiaoyang Guo; Xiaowei Zhou

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

This paper addresses the task of large-scale 3D scene reconstruction from long video sequences. Recent feed-forward reconstruction models have shown promising results by directly regressing 3D geometry from RGB images without explicit 3D priors or geometric constraints. However, these methods often struggle to maintain reconstruction accuracy and consistency over long sequences due to limited memory capacity and the inability to effectively capture global contextual cues. In contrast, humans can naturally exploit the global understanding of the scene to inform local perception. Motivated by this, we propose a novel neural global context representation that efficiently compresses and retains long-range scene information, enabling the model to leverage extensive contextual cues for enhanced reconstruction accuracy and consistency. The context representation is realized through a set of lightweight neural sub-networks that are rapidly adapted during test time via self-supervised objectives, which substantially increases memory capacity without incurring significant computational overhead. The experiments on multiple large-scale benchmarks, including the KITTI Odometry~\cite{Geiger2012CVPR} and Oxford Spires~\cite{tao2025spires} datasets, demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in handling ultra-large scenes, achieving leading pose accuracy and state-of-the-art 3D reconstruction accuracy while maintaining efficiency. Code is available at https://zju3dv.github.io/scal3r.

item.title: OmniHands: Robust Motion Capture of Interactive Hands via A Versatile Transformer
item.dc:creator: Dixuan Lin
Yuxiang Zhang
Mengcheng Li
Wei Jing
Qi Yan
Qianying Wang
Yebin Liu
Hongwen Zhang
item.description: In this paper, we introduce OmniHands, a universal approach to recovering interactive hand motions and their relative movement from monocular or multi-view inputs. Our approach addresses three major limitations of previous methods: lacking a unified solution for handling single hand and interactive hands image inputs, lacking a versatile framework to support various 4D tasks such as video-based tasks and multi-view tasks, and unable to maintain robust performance in real-world data. To overcome these challenges, we develop a universal architecture with novel tokenization and contextual feature fusion strategies, capable of adapting to a variety of tasks. Specifically, we propose a Relation-aware Two-Hand Tokenization (RAT) method to embed positional relation information into the hand tokens. In this way, our network can handle both single-hand and two-hand inputs and explicitly leverage relative hand positions, facilitating the reconstruction of intricate hand interactions in real-world scenarios. As such tokenization indicates the relative relationship of two hands, it also supports more effective feature fusion. To this end, we further develop a 4D Interaction Reasoning (FIR) module to fuse hand tokens in 4D with attention and decode them into 3D hand meshes and relative temporal movements. The efficacy of our approach is validated on several benchmark datasets. The results on in-the-wild videos and real-world scenarios demonstrate the superior performances of our approach for interactive hand reconstruction. The code and video results can be found in the supplementary materials. Published: 2026-04-11T11:03:01+00:00 Venue: ACM Transactions on Graphics Score: 0.778 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Dixuan Lin; Yuxiang Zhang; Mengcheng Li; Wei Jing; Qi Yan; Qianying Wang; Yebin Liu; Hongwen Zhang

Journal: ACM Transactions on Graphics

DOI: 10.1145/3807943

Relevance: 0.778 (must_read)

In this paper, we introduce OmniHands, a universal approach to recovering interactive hand motions and their relative movement from monocular or multi-view inputs. Our approach addresses three major limitations of previous methods: lacking a unified solution for handling single hand and interactive hands image inputs, lacking a versatile framework to support various 4D tasks such as video-based tasks and multi-view tasks, and unable to maintain robust performance in real-world data. To overcome these challenges, we develop a universal architecture with novel tokenization and contextual feature fusion strategies, capable of adapting to a variety of tasks. Specifically, we propose a Relation-aware Two-Hand Tokenization (RAT) method to embed positional relation information into the hand tokens. In this way, our network can handle both single-hand and two-hand inputs and explicitly leverage relative hand positions, facilitating the reconstruction of intricate hand interactions in real-world scenarios. As such tokenization indicates the relative relationship of two hands, it also supports more effective feature fusion. To this end, we further develop a 4D Interaction Reasoning (FIR) module to fuse hand tokens in 4D with attention and decode them into 3D hand meshes and relative temporal movements. The efficacy of our approach is validated on several benchmark datasets. The results on in-the-wild videos and real-world scenarios demonstrate the superior performances of our approach for interactive hand reconstruction. The code and video results can be found in the supplementary materials.

item.title: Symbiotic-MoE: Unlocking the Synergy between Generation and Understanding
item.dc:creator: Xiangyue Liu
Zijian Zhang
Miles Yang
Zhao Zhong
Liefeng Bo
Ping Tan
item.description: Empowering Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) with image generation often leads to catastrophic forgetting in understanding tasks due to severe gradient conflicts. While existing paradigms like Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) mitigate this conflict through structural isolation, they fundamentally sever cross-modal synergy and suffer from capacity fragmentation. In this work, we present Symbiotic-MoE, a unified pre-training framework that resolves task interference within a native multimodal Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Transformers architecture with zero-parameter overhead. We first identify that standard MoE tuning leads to routing collapse, where generative gradients dominate expert utilization. To address this, we introduce Modality-Aware Expert Disentanglement, which partitions experts into task-specific groups while utilizing shared experts as a multimodal semantic bridge. Crucially, this design allows shared experts to absorb fine-grained visual semantics from generative tasks to enrich textual representations. To optimize this, we propose a Progressive Training Strategy featuring differential learning rates and early-stage gradient shielding. This mechanism not only shields pre-trained knowledge from early volatility but eventually transforms generative signals into constructive feedback for understanding. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Symbiotic-MoE achieves rapid generative convergence while unlocking cross-modal synergy, boosting inherent understanding with remarkable gains on MMLU and OCRBench. Published: 2026-04-09T03:19:26+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.778 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiangyue Liu; Zijian Zhang; Miles Yang; Zhao Zhong; Liefeng Bo; Ping Tan

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.778 (must_read)

Empowering Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) with image generation often leads to catastrophic forgetting in understanding tasks due to severe gradient conflicts. While existing paradigms like Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) mitigate this conflict through structural isolation, they fundamentally sever cross-modal synergy and suffer from capacity fragmentation. In this work, we present Symbiotic-MoE, a unified pre-training framework that resolves task interference within a native multimodal Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Transformers architecture with zero-parameter overhead. We first identify that standard MoE tuning leads to routing collapse, where generative gradients dominate expert utilization. To address this, we introduce Modality-Aware Expert Disentanglement, which partitions experts into task-specific groups while utilizing shared experts as a multimodal semantic bridge. Crucially, this design allows shared experts to absorb fine-grained visual semantics from generative tasks to enrich textual representations. To optimize this, we propose a Progressive Training Strategy featuring differential learning rates and early-stage gradient shielding. This mechanism not only shields pre-trained knowledge from early volatility but eventually transforms generative signals into constructive feedback for understanding. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Symbiotic-MoE achieves rapid generative convergence while unlocking cross-modal synergy, boosting inherent understanding with remarkable gains on MMLU and OCRBench.

item.title: Multimodal Latent Reasoning via Predictive Embeddings
item.dc:creator: Ashutosh Adhikari
Mirella Lapata
item.description: Tool-augmented multimodal reasoning enables visual language models (VLMs) to improve perception by interacting with external tools (e.g., cropping, depth estimation). However, such approaches incur substantial inference overhead, require specialized supervision, and are prone to erroneous tool calls. We propose Pearl (Predictive Embedding Alignment for Reasoning in Latent space), a JEPA-inspired framework that learns from expert tool-use trajectories entirely in the latent space, eliminating the need for explicit tool invocation at inference time. Unlike reconstruction-based latent reasoning methods, which autoregressively generate latent tokens and suffer from training-inference mismatch and limited support for multi-step tool use, Pearl directly learns predictive embeddings from multimodal trajectories while preserving the standard vision-language generation pipeline: it is model-agnostic, simple to train, and naturally supports trajectories with multiple tool calls. Experiments across multiple perception benchmarks show that Pearl matches or outperforms standard supervised fine-tuning and reconstruction-based latent reasoning approaches. Furthermore, we provide empirical evidence that reconstruction-based methods primarily learn embeddings rather than image edits in latent space, motivating predictive embedding learning as a more principled alternative. Published: 2026-04-09T10:27:32+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.777 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ashutosh Adhikari; Mirella Lapata

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.777 (must_read)

Tool-augmented multimodal reasoning enables visual language models (VLMs) to improve perception by interacting with external tools (e.g., cropping, depth estimation). However, such approaches incur substantial inference overhead, require specialized supervision, and are prone to erroneous tool calls. We propose Pearl (Predictive Embedding Alignment for Reasoning in Latent space), a JEPA-inspired framework that learns from expert tool-use trajectories entirely in the latent space, eliminating the need for explicit tool invocation at inference time. Unlike reconstruction-based latent reasoning methods, which autoregressively generate latent tokens and suffer from training-inference mismatch and limited support for multi-step tool use, Pearl directly learns predictive embeddings from multimodal trajectories while preserving the standard vision-language generation pipeline: it is model-agnostic, simple to train, and naturally supports trajectories with multiple tool calls. Experiments across multiple perception benchmarks show that Pearl matches or outperforms standard supervised fine-tuning and reconstruction-based latent reasoning approaches. Furthermore, we provide empirical evidence that reconstruction-based methods primarily learn embeddings rather than image edits in latent space, motivating predictive embedding learning as a more principled alternative.

item.title: LPLCv2: An Expanded Dataset for Fine-Grained License Plate Legibility Classification
item.dc:creator: Lucas Wojcik
Eduardo A. F. Machoski
Eduil Nascimento
Rayson Laroca
David Menotti
item.description: Modern Automatic License Plate Recognition (ALPR) systems achieve outstanding performance in controlled, well-defined scenarios. However, large-scale real-world usage remains challenging due to low-quality imaging devices, compression artifacts, and suboptimal camera installation. Identifying illegible license plates (LPs) has recently become feasible through a dedicated benchmark; however, its impact has been limited by its small size and annotation errors. In this work, we expand the original benchmark to over three times the size with two extra capture days, revise its annotations and introduce novel labels. LP-level annotations include bounding boxes, text, and legibility level, while vehicle-level annotations comprise make, model, type, and color. Image-level annotations feature camera identity, capture conditions (e.g., rain and faulty cameras), acquisition time, and day ID. We present a novel training procedure featuring an Exponential Moving Average-based loss function and a refined learning rate scheduler, addressing common mistakes in testing. These improvements enable a baseline model to achieve an 89.5% F1-score on the test set, considerably surpassing the previous state of the art. We further introduce a novel protocol to explicitly addresses camera contamination between training and evaluation splits, where results show a small impact. Dataset and code are publicly available at https://github.com/lmlwojcik/LPLCv2-Dataset. Published: 2026-04-09T20:07:36+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.777 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lucas Wojcik; Eduardo A. F. Machoski; Eduil Nascimento; Rayson Laroca; David Menotti

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.777 (must_read)

Modern Automatic License Plate Recognition (ALPR) systems achieve outstanding performance in controlled, well-defined scenarios. However, large-scale real-world usage remains challenging due to low-quality imaging devices, compression artifacts, and suboptimal camera installation. Identifying illegible license plates (LPs) has recently become feasible through a dedicated benchmark; however, its impact has been limited by its small size and annotation errors. In this work, we expand the original benchmark to over three times the size with two extra capture days, revise its annotations and introduce novel labels. LP-level annotations include bounding boxes, text, and legibility level, while vehicle-level annotations comprise make, model, type, and color. Image-level annotations feature camera identity, capture conditions (e.g., rain and faulty cameras), acquisition time, and day ID. We present a novel training procedure featuring an Exponential Moving Average-based loss function and a refined learning rate scheduler, addressing common mistakes in testing. These improvements enable a baseline model to achieve an 89.5% F1-score on the test set, considerably surpassing the previous state of the art. We further introduce a novel protocol to explicitly addresses camera contamination between training and evaluation splits, where results show a small impact. Dataset and code are publicly available at https://github.com/lmlwojcik/LPLCv2-Dataset.
